name: LXR — RC + Immix for Low Latency at Throughput
summary: Zhao, Blackburn, McKinley's LXR (PLDI 2022) combines reference counting with Immix's mark-region heap to deliver low pauses via regular brief stop-the-world cycles plus occasional concurrent tracing for cycles — contradicting the orthodoxy that low pauses require concurrent copying.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Zhao, Blackburn, McKinley's **LXR** (PLDI 2022) combines reference counting with Immix's mark-region heap to deliver low pauses *via* regular brief stop-the-world cycles plus occasional concurrent tracing for cycles. It contradicts the orthodoxy that low pauses require concurrent copying — the design assumption underlying C4, ZGC, and Shenandoah.

LXR is the cleanest counterexample to that orthodoxy: combining RC with Immix's mark-region heap delivers low pauses via brief regular STW + occasional concurrent tracing. On Lucene with a tight heap, LXR reports 7.8x throughput and 10x better p99.99 tail latency over Shenandoah.

sections.title: Field-logging coalescing barrier and RC remembered sets
sections.content:
Field-logging **coalescing write barrier** with only ~1.6% mutator overhead, jointly maintaining RC, remembered sets, and the concurrent SATB log. **RC remembered sets** enable judicious copying of mature objects without full evacuation. **Lazy decrement processing** and survival-rate-driven pause modulation keep STW windows brief.

The mechanism leverages the fact that Immix already partitions the heap into blocks and lines: LXR layers per-object reference counts onto Immix's region structure, so most reclamation happens incrementally via RC, with periodic tracing only to handle cycles.

sections.title: Empirical results and significance
sections.content:
On Lucene with a tight heap LXR reports 7.8x throughput and 10x better p99.99 tail latency over Shenandoah. The architectural lesson: **low pause and high throughput are not in tension if the collector pays its cost incrementally rather than concurrently**. Brief regular STW + RC can hit *both* better throughput and better tail latency than concurrent copying.

Research collector implemented atop MMTk; not in production but actively developed. Status (as of 2026-04): research-grade, with continued development at ANU and Microsoft.
